sentences.text: Well, October was-- October, 1944, was the beginning of the winter.
And the winter in Poland can be very, very strong, as most of the time, as I understand.
But anyways, that had not that much bearing on it at that moment.
Because everybody was assigned to a workplace.
We-- in fact, we were shipped out after a little while, to a little side camp.
It was about-- a factory that-- that-- it was a cement factory.
Golleschau is the name, really, which is an immediate branch of Auschwitz.
They had-- all these concentration camps had branches, like a bank.
[LAUGHTER] And-- and so this was one of them.
And there we were assigned to stone quarries and other hard work.
I was fortunate, again, to-- when I let it be known that I know how to design and build furniture, they put me out again.
And marched me off to a room where there was a drawing board and some machinery, and so on, so forth.
And I was then there, again, doing the same thing that I did before.
I started to design something and then build something, as-- as it was in the previous--
Well, in the camps, I never met any of the guards that were kind to you.
If I was lucky, they ignored me.
Which was most of the time.
I tried not to show up anyway.
As I tried to keep step when we were marching.
Because that was one of their moments, that they were irked if you didn't keep step.
You know, the German military machine.
And so I tried to very much be aware of that.
People were beaten up by them when-- if-- if you would step outside the line a little bit, or something like that.
In fact, there were some cases reported-- which I didn't witness, but they were reported-- that some guys got shot that way.
You know, they went out of the line a little bit for some reason, lost their balance or what-- who knows what, and were shot that way.
They claimed they tried to escape.
No, it never happened to me, fortunately.
I was constantly aware of that, unfortunately.
And I controlled it.
But I was soon, as I said-- oh yeah.
Then when I was assigned to-- to-- to the little workshop, there the guard came in with me.
The guard marched me off with his dog.
And that dog was a beautiful, huge shepherd.
But the moment the guard would talk to me, the dog would get up and growl.
I started to almost-- to hate dogs.
I got over that later on.
But the guards would never talk to me, unless it was work related.
So there I was.
And as I said, I never met any friendly guard.
Like this guard, he just ignored me otherwise.
Didn't do anything.

Unless, when I sometimes was designing, and I looked up and tried to think about which way to go with my pencil now, and-- and stopping for a moment and thinking, that was the moment he jumped in and said, hey, Jew, you're not supposed to stand and dream.
You go back to work.
There was no use to explaining to these guys that, hey, I need a moment to think.
So I started-- I-- I put my pencil a little bit in kind of movement, whether-- whether I actually was doing something that didn't make any difference.
This-- this guy was standing there all day long.
One of the super members of the Aryan race, the master race.
And he was standing there all day long.
Once in a while, probably, he had to show that he was a human being.
He had to go to the bathroom.
He was a human being, apparently.
And so he-- at which time he would-- this was a factory otherwise.
I had my own little room.
But there was a factory.
They-- they made barrels, wooden barrels.
For-- for butter, in those days, I would think they were used, other things like that.
But-- and they were all Polish-- Polish workers in there.
They were under the control of the Nazis.
They were also under slavery conditions in there.
And so when he left, he would call one of the Polish foremen in.
And he stood there instead-- in space of the guard.
Supposed to guard me, that I don't run out or something, or what.
Who knows what?
When the guard was gone, I was always glad to be able to close the door.
For some reason, the guard always wanted the door open.
But when the guard was gone, I wasn't so much afraid of that Polish worker.
I went and closed the door.
Well, that Polish worker who spoke very little German, apparently, opened that door again.
And I went and closed it again.
Until this guy said, hey, something like, are you stupid?
Leave that door open.
And in this way of saying, there was some sort of a message in there, I guess.
And sure enough, I open the door.
Suddenly somebody walks quickly by outside the door.
And a ball-- and-- and-- and an apple rolls over the floor, like a ball, toward-- towards my workbench.
Or a piece of bread was just thrown in like a baseball, you know, towards my direction.
And so this-- this-- this was the reason why this Polish worker wanted me to keep the door open.
So they tried to help me.
It was a wonderful thing.
And of course, then I tried to smuggle it back.
Parts of it, at least.
I ate, of course.
And parts of it I smuggled back into the camp, in order to be able to share something with my brother.
Oh.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
And-- and this was-- a piece of bread was the most prized possession that you could have under those circumstances.
And I smuggled it in.
And if they would have caught me, with smuggling this in, it would have been the end of me.
No, I did the same thing, like I did with the tomatoes.
Yeah?
So-- so wrapped in a rag, and fastened between your legs.
And so talking about the prized possession of bread.

At one time, my brother found a student, a former-- my brother was a teacher before-- a student of his that worked out in-- for the guards, and at one time brought my brother a piece of bread that he had been able to bring in.
Stolen or what, I don't know.
And my brother took-- took-- took the piece of bread and-- and-- and-- and gave it to me as my birthday present.
It just happened to be my birthday the next day.
And I wanted to celebrate my birthday by eating that piece of bread for breakfast.
Because you remember, we didn't get anything for breakfast.
That black water was all we got.
And then we went out to work.
And I wanted to eat that piece.
I wanted to celebrate.
And I wanted to eat that piece of bread for breakfast.
And I put it under what was sort of my pillow, a little straw cover there, like that.
And I put it underneath it under the bunk.
And-- and I-- I think I probably put my head on it.
Even at that time, you didn't think of any-- you know, you had no qualms about anything.
And so-- but the morning-- and that was-- that was really something I can never forget.
You're talking about hurt feelings.
Nothing was hurting me more than the next morning when that thing had been stolen, that piece of bread.
I remember that.
That was-- that was terrible.
So-- because my brother would-- would have needed that piece of bread himself, for his survival, you know.
But he wanted me to have it.
And so-- so that piece of bread was stolen.
So it's just a little episode of-- from Auschwitz.
And so now anyways, so we were working away.
My brother had to work very, very hard in the stone quarries.
Unfortunately I was able to take it as much as possible.
Then eventually-- this was Golleschau, really, that side camp, the cement factory.
Then, you know, I think a very interesting little story I should probably bring out, a story of survival.
I mentioned, briefly, only that it was a very important thing for us that we were together.
Because we watched each others.
If either one would-- of us-- go off the deep end and say, hey, what's the use?
You know, we're not going to make it.
Look, these people around us are dying and being picked out to die, and stuff like that.
Then one of us would say, hey, don't be silly.
It's just a little while away.
The Allies will be here.
And you know, just hang in there.
This-- this usual thing, you know.
And so we caught each other, doing that.
Now, this camp, this Golleschau, this cement factory, was a huge brick building complex.
There was a big square yard surrounded by four-story building, brick building.
Four-story brick building.
And so there was, in the one corner, there was a shower.
Every once in a while we were allowed to have that shower.
We were ordered to go down in the shower.
And that was a wonderful thing.
Just lasted maybe for a couple of minutes.
But it was a nice hot shower.
And we came out.
And you're talking about the winter, I'm talking now, by that time, what I'm specifically recall was a winter.
There was snow on the ground. on the-- on the-- on this court-- and this courtyard was just-- was covered with snow.
We are coming out of the shower.
And we are standing naked, wet.
We didn't receive any towel.
Nor, by the way, did we receive any soap.
Just hot water, which was wonderful.
But then we came out, now, in the winter.
Snow on the ground, and we are standing there, wet in line to wait for our clothing.
New clothing, the old stuff we had to drop before we went in.
And so we wait for our clothing.
And now it's ice cold.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
I'm sorry.
Oh, I'm sorry.
I-- I forgot to unplug it.
Unplug it.
--afterwards.
OK,
Got to-- got to take a break now.
Unplug it, Stephie.
STEPHANIE (GRANDDAUGHTER): Yeah.
So, anyways so there we-- we-- we are standing there, waiting for our clothes-- clothing.
And it was ice cold.
And people would stand there and-- and shiver, rightfully, and say, oh my god, now we are going to get the pneumonia.
This is all we need.
That's impossible.
We can't last anymore.
And these-- these people with that attitude, of course, had a problem.
And-- and while it's understandable, I'm not trying to criticize it.
But my brother and I were watching each other.
And we would-- we were not allowed to run around.
But you could stomp on the spot if you wanted to.
And we would rub each other's back.
And then-- and then we would say, hey, listen.
Isn't this wonderful?
The snow, the hot bath.
Isn't it just like the Swedish baths are?
This is supposed to be healthy.
And that we'll be healthy, and that will be helping us.
People around us looked like as if we were nuts.
You know, they-- they went, hey, what are these guys talking?
And so this is one of the things.
Then eventually, it took a long time.
Because these guards that-- guards that handed out the clothing had their drinks and-- and their cigarettes.
And they would take all their time, would slap each others on the shoulder in their big fat coats, and took all the time in the world to hand the clothing out.
Just to give you a little scene of part of survival, I guess.
Anyways, as the Russians came closer, now towards the end.
I am talking by now it's January, beginning of January, 1945.
The Russians from the East had thrown the Germans back, finally, further and further towards German-- German soil, towards Germany.
So of course they came towards Auschwitz, which is located in Poland.
And as the Russians came closer, there was a danger, quote, unquote, 'danger' for the Nazis that we would be liberated.
We were not supposed to be liberated.
By the way, also to interject very quickly, Auschwitz was the largest extermination camp of all the camps.
It is estimated that between 1 and 1/2, closer to 2 million probably, people were killed there.
It's estimated, and on my part, the estimations that I read, probably like 3/4 millions were Poles, whom by that-- by that time Hitler had declared also subhumans.
They were killed in Auschwitz, about, let's say, 3/4 million of them.
About 120 to 150,000 gypsies, whom of course Hitler had declared subhumans.
A little more, I believe, than a million Jews, somewhat more than a million Jews.
And a rough estimate, out of these Jews, there were probably half a million or a little bit more just children.
Children that had committed the big crime of being born Jewish.
So it was the most horrible killing machine that ever was invented by, I am hesitant to say, human beings.
Well, as the Russians came closer, we were then ordered-- we were not allowed to survive, not allowed to be rescued.
We were then ordered out into marching columns.
And we were involved in what's called to be a death march.
I will come, really, to the details of a death march a little later.
Because there was a second one that I was in.
And they are ?] identical to each other.
That I'll-- don't describe this in detail here.

We were marched through snow and ice for about three days, where probably nearly half of the people that were involved in that march had succumbed-- had died, in the way that I will explain later, mostly.
Many of them just collapsed and died, of course, in the ice and snow.
Many of them didn't have any shoes anymore.
And ours were very, very bad and torn, and so frostbite was the [? order-- ?]
From the camp towards a train station.
And-- and-- in former Southeast Germany, Gleiwitz area.
It's a town.
And so, after three days, then we were loaded up onto a-- onto a train.
And we were taken out of there, out of that area.
We were taken into a camp called Sachsenhausen, by Berlin.
In fact, we drove right by the old area of that.
By the way, this time the train was not even covered.
This time it was open box cars.
And each prisoner had one blanket.
But we were-- the box was loaded full, that actually you were sitting right next to each other.
And even-- and that way with the blankets, you were not so tremendously cold.
But it was bad enough anyways.
I remember also, we stopped, at one time, right next to a German military train.
And since these were open box cars, of course we stood up and looked over-- over the border of it.
A German military train, right next to us on the track.
And it happened to be that right on our wagon, where-- where we-- where our wagon was there was, on that military train, a huge, huge kettle with something liquid.
As it turned out, it was apparently coffee.
Because the guy filled up big cans and-- and handed them to other German soldiers that carried them away somewhere.
Then he was done with it.
Nobody was there.
And we had our-- our-- our eating dishes with us.
That was always with us, hanging us, on the string-- on the stringy belt that we had.
And we would hang-- hold our dish over towards this thing, that this soldier could easily have filled up-- filled up one of those big carafes, or what he had, and poured it in to us.
And he we all held it out to have some of this hot stuff, which was probably coffee, or whatever it was.
He-- he looked-- at-- at one time he looked at us straight, opened the spigot of that big kettle, and let it run out right in front of us, onto the tracks.
We stayed there for probably 10 days, two weeks.
The commander of that camp-- his-- his camp was overcrowded already.
Because all other areas of-- of outlying camps, the-- the Germans-- the Nazis-- put us into-- into Germany.
And so these guys wanted to get rid of us as soon as possible, obviously.
And so we were then taken to another camp, the Heinkelwerke, which was a factory, an airplane factory.
And there too, for a few days only.
Then we were loaded up again.
And we were taken to a camp called Flossenburg, which is located in northern-- Northeast Bavaria.
Flossenburg was a camp designed to hold 5,000 prisoners.
When we arrived there, there were about 140,000 there.
At that time, though, we were not assigned to any working place anymore.
We would be kept busy in head counts, from morning to evening.
Prisoners just dropped on the spot.
And it was an automatic thing.

The prisoners next them would have to carry them away, putting them on big body piles, who then eventually would be thrown onto some car-- cars, trucks, and would be taken to the crematorium to be burned.
Nothing else happened there.
That's the way it was.
Except I developed something very, very miserable.
I developed a-- a growth under my left arm here, that started to grow and grow.
And-- and eventually developed into the size of-- of an orange, big orange, grapefruit almost.
And so I couldn't put my arm close to me.
And it started, also, to invade my body.
I got-- became feverish and sick feeling.
And sometimes I couldn't stand up straight.
And my brother was with me, who helped me standing on my feet.
Because it would have been dangerous otherwise.
And so he was always with me.
When we were driven back into the barracks he was behind me.
Because when we were driven in, we were driven in, literally by-- by with beatings, gun butts.
Always hollered, faster, faster, faster, they hollered, and would beat us.
And so-- and I couldn't move in between the people who were clamping together in front of a narrow door.
Because I would faint when-- when my arm would be squeezed too hard.
And so I stood at the end of it, rather taking the beating.
But my brother would stand in-- in back of me, and would catch all this.
And so eventually, we came into the barracks that way.
And suddenly it came out that-- as I say, I was fairly helpless.
My brother was constantly with me there.

And it certainly-- and the order was given that they are looking for 1,000 men that would be able to be mustered out for a work commando, to be taken out of the camp, not too far away, where they intend to build an airstrip.
You see, the Nazis had been driven in from all sides.
In the meantime, lost airfields, and stuff like that.
And of course we're still fighting.
And they needed more airfields.
And so they started to muster out a thousand men.
And my brother said to me, look, you've got to try to hide this thing.
Because you've got to come with me.
Because if we are separated, you know very well your situation is very, very hopeless this way.
Which-- which was very cool.
And I knew it.
I was fully aware of that.
I had no chance to survive for any length of time.
But anyways, so we went by the doctors, I tried to hide this thing, but the doctors noticed.
And they didn't-- they sent my brother this way and kept me on the side of being kept in the camp.
Which turned out to be the most disastrous happening for us all the time when we were in the camp.
Yet, as you will see, that was, for us, the only way to survive, as it turns out to be.
Anyways, well, I can come to the reason right away.
It turns out to be that my brother is the only survivor of this 1,000-man commando.
This 1,000-men commando was down to a size-- to a size, after four weeks, of 52 people.
And my brother was able to escape the last moment, during one night.
And he was taken in by a German farmer, who was obviously no Nazi.
Because he could tell whom he had in front of him.
And he took him down in the basement.
And the next day the farmer came to him and he said, say, do you-- were you with another group of about 52 prisoners?
And my brother said, oh yes, yes.
He says, well, he says, then you are lucky that you came here.
Because these 52 prisoners were all put before a machine gun, and all shot over-- all shot.
So this is the way.
Now, under these circumstances, the condition that I was, there would have been no chance for both of us to survive.
So you see, sometimes in life, the thing that you think is the worst that happens to you turns out to be a-- a lucky break.
As it turns out then, when my brother was taken away from me, I-- This is an incredible-- incredible story.
And I-- I will tell that.
It will take a few minutes.
So now my brother was away from me.
I took inventory of my situation very, very clearly.
By that time I knew I had no chance, unless I do something drastic, which I hope will work.
And our blockalteste, the commander of the barrack, so to speak, happened to be, in this particular case, a man who hated Jews with a fashion.
He was a man from the Ukraine.
I'm not trying to say that Ukrainians are all hating Jews.
A good number do, of course.
But then there are also some wonderful Ukrainians, even known, nowadays, that actually helped Jews, so-- But this man hated Jews with a fashion.
Any time he had a chance, he would-- would beat up on us, would beat people to a pulp, prisoners to a pulp.
Now that my brother was gone, I need-- I knew I needed medical attention, or medical help.
Which was in existence.
The weird thing is that it was there.
It was usually available for people that were maybe in some privileged position, people that worked somewhat-- somewhat with the Nazis, or worked in the kitchen, or something like that.
And I didn't think I had a chance.
I approached this blockalteste and said, look, I need-- I need to go-- to be allowed to go to the medical barracks.
He didn't answer me.
The only thing is, I had his fist in my face.
Broke my glasses.
And of course, besides the pain, and that-- that was his answer.
So but of course, my glasses I could replace.
Because in the corner of the barracks was a huge, huge bin with all glasses put in there from people that had died.
And so therefore, I was able to scrounge around in there until I found one that would fit my eye condition closest.
And so that was it.
So now I knew I couldn't ask this man again.
Because it was impossible.
And so I-- I developed a plan.
I have to get out of there.
I haven't got a chance to survive.
I have to get help.
So I decided to sneak out in the early morning when this guy-- this guy, fortunately, was snoring when he was sleeping, always.
We could hear him all through the barracks.
He had his preferred corner there, with a little better bed, and his own bed and stuff like that.
Then we in our bunks.
And he was snoring.
And while he was snoring in the morning, it was still dark, I went.
And he was laying next to the door, too, close to the door.
I snuck out.
And finally got by him.
The door, even, was creaking a little bit.
It was dangerous.
And I went down.
And I was on my way to the-- to the sick barracks.
And as I came close, there was a long line of people standing with their little pink slips in their hand, which I should have had from this guy if he would have been-- given it to me.
And so I stand in line.
And very, very long line.
It took a long time.
These guys were never in a hurry anyway, the-- the officials.
And so little by little, I worked my way that way, towards the entrance door.
And at the entrance door there was an SS man with his helper.
And he would take those pink slips from these-- from these prisoners and then get them in through the door.
And as I get closer, I get more and more worried.
Because what are you going to tell those guys?
You know, you can't get by with, oh my gosh, I must have lost it on the way.
You can't.
I can't get by with this.
So I was really getting worried.
In fact, if I'm lucky they just turn me away instead of beating me.
They turn me away and send me back to my barracks.
How-- by now it got bright.
And everybody I know was up.
How do I get by this guy who sat, most of the day, right by the door?
You know, you couldn't pass him.
And I'm starting to get worried.
How will this work?
I don't have the pink slip.
And I get in.
And as I get closer and closer, there's one guy in front of me yet.
And he takes the slip from this guy.
And while he takes the slip away from this guy, another SS man comes from somewhere and taps this guard on the shoulder.
And he and his helper both turn-- turn around to face this guard that tapped him on the shoulder.
And I just snuck in at that moment.
I mean, you can't plan it that way.
It's a miracle that happens.
So now there I am, inside.
My problems aren't over yet.
But I'm inside and I'm being-- there's one guy that looks at what-- what the reason is for your being there.
And he sees that swelling.
And he sends me to a line.
And I'm-- in front of me there are maybe five or six other prisoners with similar things.
They have these swellings on the-- on the-- on our chest, on our legs, all over the place, similar things like that.
And there I see this-- this doctor.
An SS man in-- in a general's rank.
Schmitz ?] was his name.
I can never forget it.

He takes care of these people, quote, unquote, 'takes care.' He stands there in his white smock, and has two huge helpers that take these-- the guy that comes in, take those guys like, in a-- in a vise, by their arms.
And this doctor just takes the scalpel and just cuts them open, slits them open.
Takes a paper towel, wipes it off, puts it down, until next guy comes.
Now, I noticed-- of course, naturally these guys were hollering and yelling.
And the moment they were hollering he took the fist and hit them.
And so in fact, I noticed on couple of other guys, that when they were asking-- these were all wretched souls by now, OK?
These were all so long in prison, and wretched and sick souls.
And they would start off begging a little bit, like.
Oh doctor, please help me, I don't want to die.
And naturally I don't, by any means, mean to mean fun of it.
Don't get me wrong at all here.
These were wretched souls that felt-- that hoped that this guy has a little bit of humanity in him.
And I noticed, whenever they would whine like that, this guy got mad, this doctor.
And he would just hit them in the face or in the belly, or anything like that.
And these-- these helpers would hold the guy by that time.
And I noticed-- for-- there were two things that were in my favor.
Number one, I was the last in-- in line.
And number two, I really had my brain working very, very much.
And I noticed that this guy doesn't like the whining.
And so I figured when my turn comes, I better speak up, like in the military, German military-like fashion.
And it came to that, when I-- when-- when I-- I came forward to him.
These guys were holding me right away.
And I-- and he says, well, what's the matter with you?
Like he'd ask anybody.
And these other guys would start whining, some of them.
And I said, well, Herr General, please look at this.
And he looked.
In a very snappy form, I said that.
Not snappy, like that.
This wasn't snappy, the way I said it.
At that time a very snappy form.
And he looks at me like he sees a miracle.
And he puts his hand on my shoulder and says, OK, my boy.
I'm going to put you to sleep.
Which I have heard before many, many times.
And-- and I knew the connotation of it.
But I had no choice.
There I was.
And he put me-- even on the table.
He told those guys you put him down on the table.
He put me on the table, which he never did with the others.
And I got this mask.
At that time, the ether mask they used.
And he really put me to sleep.
And I'm being-- I'm being awakened with some slaps on my hand and my face.
That was the case to do it, not hitting, slapping, to awake people from that kind of ether anesthesia.
And I got off the table.
And now I'm wondering, oh my god.
And I noticed I had a big bandage around here.
And I said, my gosh, how do I get back to my barracks?
What am I going to do now?
How can I sneak by there?
Because against this guy's orders.
You know, it was a death sentence.
Absolutely.
I've seen them do that.
And at this moment, this-- this Nazi doctor says to the guys, hey, see to it that he stays here in the sick barracks.
And so I stayed down there.
And as it turns out, I gained my strength.
By the way, I stayed down there and they put me in one of the bunk beds.
I was very skinny, like all the prisoners were.
In one of the bunk beds, the same bunk beds that you see sometimes for children, there were three guys laying in each bunk.
One this way, one that way, and one that way.
And we were covered with a blanket that was filthy from dried pus, similar things like this.
Because my bandage was a paper bandage that lasted about two weeks.
Ah-- two weeks?
Two-- two hours, I mean.
And then it was all soaked through.
There was no stitching at that time.
Or either it wasn't available, or they didn't do it.
I don't know anymore.
So I was just vulnerable to any kind of infection.
And it's another miracle that it didn't happen.
I talked to my doctor once, who-- who gave me a shot and wiped it off, you know, with-- first with alcohol, with a little needle.
They said, you know something?
Nothing was wiped off when this thing was done.
It was just cut open.
And so he says well, those things do happen, he says, you don't always get infected.
So this was it.
I gained my strength.

And I was able and at one time, even ordered to-- to get a little-- to do a little job, to cut hair somewhere-- some-- for new-arriving prisoners, for which I got a little bowl of soup, which was all fine and wonderful.
And this was the way I was there, until-- until we finally were ordered out of the camp.
OK.
